title: The Shadow of a Bicycle
text_zh: 楼下停车棚里有一辆自行车，已经至少两年没人骑了。轮胎早就瘪了，链条也生了锈，车篮里积了一层灰。但每天下午，阳光照进停车棚的时候，这辆自行车的影子会被拉得很长，投在地面上，看起来像一匹正在奔跑的马。我第一次注意到这件事的时候觉得很有意思：一辆完全静止的、被遗忘的自行车，它的影子却在「奔跑」。当然，这只是光线角度造成的视觉效果。但我忍不住想，也许每个被放弃的东西，在某个特定的时刻，都还保留着一种运动的记忆。后来有一天，物业的人来清理停车棚，把那辆自行车搬走了。第二天下午，阳光还是照了进来，但地面上只剩下一片空白。我站在那里看了一会儿，突然有一种说不清的失落感。不是为那辆自行车本身，而是为它的影子——那匹每天下午都在奔跑的、没有人注意的马。
text_pinyin: Lóu xià tíngchēpéng lǐ yǒu yī liàng zìxíngchē, yǐjīng zhìshǎo liǎng nián méi rén qí le. Lúntāi zǎo jiù biě le, liàntiáo yě shēng le xiù, chēlán lǐ jī le yī céng huī. Dàn měi tiān xiàwǔ, yángguāng zhào jìn tíngchēpéng de shíhou, zhè liàng zìxíngchē de yǐngzi huì bèi lā de hěn cháng, tóu zài dìmiàn shàng, kàn qǐlái xiàng yī pǐ zhèngzài bēnpǎo de mǎ. Wǒ dì yī cì zhùyì dào zhè jiàn shì de shíhou juéde hěn yǒu yìsi: yī liàng wánquán jìngzhǐ de, bèi yíwàng de zìxíngchē, tā de yǐngzi què zài 「bēnpǎo」. Dāngrán, zhè zhǐshì guāngxiàn jiǎodù zào chéng de shìjué xiàoguǒ. Dàn wǒ rěn bú zhù xiǎng, yěxǔ měi gè bèi fàngqì de dōngxi, zài mǒu gè tèdìng de shíkè, dōu hái bǎoliú zhe yī zhǒng yùndòng de jìyì. Hòulái yǒu yī tiān, wùyè de rén lái qīnglǐ tíngchēpéng, bǎ nà liàng zìxíngchē bān zǒu le. Dì'èr tiān xiàwǔ, yángguāng háishi zhào le jìnlái, dàn dìmiàn shàng zhǐ shèng xià yī piàn kòngbái. Wǒ zhàn zài nàlǐ kàn le yīhuìr, tūrán yǒu yī zhǒng shuō bù qīng de shīluògǎn. Bú shì wèi nà liàng zìxíngchē běnshēn, ér shì wèi tā de yǐngzi——nà pǐ měi tiān xiàwǔ dōu zài bēnpǎo de, méiyǒu rén zhùyì de mǎ.
text_en: In the bicycle shed downstairs there's a bicycle that nobody has ridden for at least two years. The tires have long gone flat, the chain has rusted, and a layer of dust has gathered in the basket. But every afternoon, when sunlight enters the shed, this bicycle's shadow stretches long across the floor, looking like a galloping horse. The first time I noticed this I found it fascinating: a completely motionless, forgotten bicycle, yet its shadow was 'running.' Of course, this is just a visual effect caused by the angle of light. But I couldn't help thinking that maybe every abandoned thing, at some particular moment, still retains a memory of movement. Then one day, the property management people came to clear the shed and took the bicycle away. The next afternoon, sunlight still streamed in, but the ground was just an empty blank. I stood there looking for a while and suddenly felt an indescribable sense of loss. Not for the bicycle itself, but for its shadow—that horse that ran every afternoon, noticed by nobody.

title: The Missing Cup
text_zh: 我们办公室有一个谜团，持续了半年才解开。事情是这样的：茶水间的柜子里本来有十二个白色陶瓷杯子，是公司统一买的。但从某一天起，杯子开始一个一个消失。一周少一个，非常有规律。到第八周的时候，只剩下四个了。大家开始互相猜测：是有人拿回家了？还是被打碎了但没人承认？行政部的小刘甚至在柜子旁边贴了一张告示：「请归还公共杯子。」没有任何效果。后来我无意中发现了答案。有一天加班很晚，我路过保洁阿姨的休息室，看见架子上整整齐齐地放着八个白色陶瓷杯子，每个杯子上都贴着一小条胶带，写着不同的名字——都是保洁阿姨们自己的名字。原来她们一直用纸杯喝水，觉得浪费，就每人拿了一个陶瓷杯。她们不知道这些杯子是公司为员工买的，以为是没人要的。我站在那里犹豫了很久，最后什么都没说，回去跟小刘说：「别找了，那些杯子在该在的地方。」
text_pinyin: Wǒmen bàngōngshì yǒu yī gè mítuán, chíxù le bànnián cái jiě kāi. Shìqíng shì zhèyàng de: cháshuǐjiān de guìzi lǐ běnlái yǒu shí'èr gè báisè táocí bēizi, shì gōngsī tǒngyī mǎi de. Dàn cóng mǒu yī tiān qǐ, bēizi kāishǐ yī gè yī gè xiāoshī. Yī zhōu shǎo yī gè, fēicháng yǒu guīlǜ. Dào dì bā zhōu de shíhou, zhǐ shèng xià sì gè le. Dàjiā kāishǐ hùxiāng cāicè: shì yǒu rén ná huí jiā le? Háishi bèi dǎsuì le dàn méi rén chéngrèn? Xíngzhèng bù de xiǎo Liú shènzhì zài guìzi pángbiān tiē le yī zhāng gàoshi: 「Qǐng guīhuán gōnggòng bēizi.」Méiyǒu rènhé xiàoguǒ. Hòulái wǒ wúyìzhōng fāxiàn le dá'àn. Yǒu yī tiān jiābān hěn wǎn, wǒ lùguò bǎojié āyí de xiūxīshì, kànjiàn jiàzi shàng zhěngzhěng qíqí de fàng zhe bā gè báisè táocí bēizi, měi gè bēizi shàng dōu tiē zhe yī xiǎo tiáo jiāodài, xiě zhe bùtóng de míngzì——dōu shì bǎojié āyímen zìjǐ de míngzì. Yuánlái tāmen yīzhí yòng zhǐbēi hē shuǐ, juéde làngfèi, jiù měi rén ná le yī gè táocí bēi. Tāmen bù zhīdào zhèxiē bēizi shì gōngsī wèi yuángōng mǎi de, yǐwéi shì méi rén yào de. Wǒ zhàn zài nàlǐ yóuyù le hěn jiǔ, zuìhòu shénme dōu méi shuō, huíqù gēn xiǎo Liú shuō: 「Bié zhǎo le, nàxiē bēizi zài gāi zài de dìfang.」
text_en: Our office had a mystery that took half a year to solve. Here's what happened: the cabinet in the tea room originally had twelve white ceramic cups, purchased by the company. But starting from a certain day, the cups began disappearing one by one. One per week, very regularly. By the eighth week, only four remained. Everyone speculated: had someone taken them home? Or were they broken but nobody confessed? Xiao Liu from admin even posted a notice by the cabinet: 'Please return the communal cups.' It had no effect. Later I discovered the answer by accident. Working late one night, I passed by the cleaning ladies' break room and saw eight white ceramic cups arranged neatly on a shelf, each with a small strip of tape bearing a different name—the cleaning ladies' own names. It turned out they'd always been drinking water from paper cups and felt it was wasteful, so each took one ceramic cup. They didn't know the cups were bought for employees—they assumed nobody wanted them. I stood there hesitating for a long time. In the end I said nothing, went back, and told Xiao Liu: 'Stop looking. Those cups are where they should be.'

title: The Neighbor's Piano
text_zh: 楼上住着一个学钢琴的小女孩，每天晚上七点到八点是她的练琴时间。刚开始的时候，我能听到的只是反反复复的音阶练习，同一个音符弹错了就重来，有时候同一小节要重复十几遍。那段时间我觉得有点吵，甚至想过去敲门请他们把窗户关上。但不知道从什么时候起，音阶练习变少了，取而代之的是完整的曲子。虽然弹得还不算流畅，但已经能听出旋律了。再后来，错音越来越少，节奏越来越稳，有一天晚上我正在看书，突然意识到自己已经不自觉地跟着她弹的曲子在心里哼了起来。那一刻我觉得很不可思议：一个我从未见过面的邻居，通过一堵墙和一架钢琴，在不知不觉中成了我生活的一部分。更让我感动的是那个过程——从笨拙的音阶到流畅的曲子，我像一个隐形的听众，见证了她整整一年的成长。后来有一天晚上，钢琴声突然停了。连续一个星期都没有声音。我这才发现，我已经习惯了那个声音，就像习惯了窗外的风声和雨声一样自然。
text_pinyin: Lóu shàng zhù zhe yī gè xué gāngqín de xiǎo nǚhái, měi tiān wǎnshang qī diǎn dào bā diǎn shì tā de liàn qín shíjiān. Gāng kāishǐ de shíhou, wǒ néng tīng dào de zhǐshì fǎnfǎn fùfù de yīnjiē liànxí, tóngyī gè yīnfú tán cuò le jiù chóng lái, yǒu shíhou tóngyī xiǎojié yào chóngfù shí jǐ biàn. Nà duàn shíjiān wǒ juéde yǒudiǎn chǎo, shènzhì xiǎng guòqù qiāo mén qǐng tāmen bǎ chuānghù guān shàng. Dàn bù zhīdào cóng shénme shíhou qǐ, yīnjiē liànxí biàn shǎo le, qǔ ér dài zhī de shì wánzhěng de qǔzi. Suīrán tán de hái bú suàn liúchàng, dàn yǐjīng néng tīng chū xuánlǜ le. Zài hòulái, cuòyīn yuè lái yuè shǎo, jiézòu yuè lái yuè wěn, yǒu yī tiān wǎnshang wǒ zhèngzài kàn shū, tūrán yìshi dào zìjǐ yǐjīng bù zìjué de gēn zhe tā tán de qǔzi zài xīnlǐ hēng le qǐlái. Nà yī kè wǒ juéde hěn bùkěsīyì: yī gè wǒ cóng wèi jiàn guò miàn de línjū, tōngguò yī dǔ qiáng hé yī jià gāngqín, zài bùzhībùjué zhōng chéng le wǒ shēnghuó de yī bùfen. Gèng ràng wǒ gǎndòng de shì nàge guòchéng——cóng bènzhuō de yīnjiē dào liúchàng de qǔzi, wǒ xiàng yī gè yǐnxíng de tīngzhòng, jiànzhèng le tā zhěngzhěng yī nián de chéngzhǎng. Hòulái yǒu yī tiān wǎnshang, gāngqín shēng tūrán tíng le. Liánxù yī gè xīngqī dōu méiyǒu shēngyīn. Wǒ zhè cái fāxiàn, wǒ yǐjīng xíguàn le nàge shēngyīn, jiù xiàng xíguàn le chuāng wài de fēngshēng hé yǔshēng yīyàng zìrán.
text_en: Upstairs lives a little girl learning piano. Every evening from seven to eight is her practice time. At first, all I could hear was repetitive scale exercises—the same note played wrong then started over, sometimes the same measure repeated a dozen times. During that period I found it a bit noisy, even thought about going upstairs to knock and ask them to close the window. But at some point I couldn't identify, the scale exercises grew fewer, replaced by complete pieces. Though not yet smooth, I could make out melodies. Then the wrong notes became rarer and rarer, the rhythm steadier and steadier, until one evening while reading I suddenly realized I was unconsciously humming along to the piece she was playing. In that moment I felt something remarkable: a neighbor I had never met, through a wall and a piano, had imperceptibly become part of my life. What moved me even more was the process—from clumsy scales to flowing pieces, I had been an invisible audience, witnessing an entire year of her growth. Then one evening, the piano suddenly stopped. For a whole week there was no sound. Only then did I discover that I had grown accustomed to that sound, as naturally as the wind and rain outside the window.

title: The Night Shift Security Guard's Notebook
text_zh: 我们写字楼的夜班保安姓孙，五十多岁，每天晚上六点到早上六点值班。大部分时间他就坐在大厅的前台后面，面前放着一个蓝色的笔记本。我一直以为那是值班记录，直到有一天深夜我加班出来，好奇地问了一句。老孙有点不好意思地把笔记本翻给我看。里面记的全是诗——不是什么名人的诗，是他自己写的。有的写夜里大厅的灯光，有的写凌晨三点的安静，有的写窗外的月亮。字迹不太好看，但每一首都认认真真地写了日期。他说他初中毕业就出来打工了，一直喜欢写东西但从来没有机会。当了夜班保安以后，终于有了大把的安静时间。「白天太吵了，什么都写不出来，」他说，「只有夜里，这个楼安静得像一条船，我就觉得心里的话都浮上来了。」我问他写了多少首了，他翻了翻说，大概三百多首。三百多首，全都写在同一个蓝色笔记本里，从来没有给任何人看过。我问他为什么不发到网上，他摇了摇头说：「写诗不是为了给别人看的，是为了陪自己度过那些安静的夜晚。」
text_pinyin: Wǒmen xiězìlóu de yèbān bǎo'ān xìng Sūn, wǔshí duō suì, měi tiān wǎnshang liù diǎn dào zǎoshang liù diǎn zhíbān. Dàbùfen shíjiān tā jiù zuò zài dàtīng de qiántái hòumiàn, miànqián fàng zhe yī gè lánsè de bǐjìběn. Wǒ yīzhí yǐwéi nà shì zhíbān jìlù, zhídào yǒu yī tiān shēnyè wǒ jiābān chūlái, hàoqí de wèn le yī jù. Lǎo Sūn yǒudiǎn bù hǎoyìsi de bǎ bǐjìběn fān gěi wǒ kàn. Lǐmiàn jì de quán shì shī——bú shì shénme míngrén de shī, shì tā zìjǐ xiě de. Yǒu de xiě yèlǐ dàtīng de dēngguāng, yǒu de xiě língchén sān diǎn de ānjìng, yǒu de xiě chuāng wài de yuèliang. Zìjì bú tài hǎokàn, dàn měi yī shǒu dōu rènrènzhēnzhēn de xiě le rìqī. Tā shuō tā chūzhōng bìyè jiù chūlái dǎgōng le, yīzhí xǐhuan xiě dōngxi dàn cónglái méiyǒu jīhuì. Dāng le yèbān bǎo'ān yǐhòu, zhōngyú yǒu le dà bǎ de ānjìng shíjiān. 「Báitiān tài chǎo le, shénme dōu xiě bù chūlái,」tā shuō, 「zhǐyǒu yèlǐ, zhège lóu ānjìng de xiàng yī tiáo chuán, wǒ jiù juéde xīnlǐ de huà dōu fú shàng lái le.」Wǒ wèn tā xiě le duōshao shǒu le, tā fān le fān shuō, dàgài sānbǎi duō shǒu. Sānbǎi duō shǒu, quándōu xiě zài tóngyī gè lánsè bǐjìběn lǐ, cónglái méiyǒu gěi rènhé rén kàn guò. Wǒ wèn tā wèishéme bù fā dào wǎng shàng, tā yáo le yáo tóu shuō: 「Xiě shī bú shì wèile gěi biéren kàn de, shì wèile péi zìjǐ dùguò nàxiē ānjìng de yèwǎn.」
text_en: The night-shift security guard at our office building is surnamed Sun, in his fifties, on duty from 6 PM to 6 AM every day. Most of the time he just sits behind the front desk in the lobby, a blue notebook in front of him. I always assumed it was the duty log, until one late night when I came out after working overtime and asked out of curiosity. Old Sun, a bit embarrassed, flipped the notebook open for me. It was full of poetry—not famous poets' work, but his own. Some described the lobby lights at night, some the silence at 3 AM, some the moon outside the window. The handwriting wasn't pretty, but every poem had a date carefully noted. He said he'd gone out to work after finishing middle school and had always liked writing but never had the chance. After becoming a night security guard, he finally had vast stretches of quiet time. 'Daytime is too noisy—I can't write anything,' he said. 'Only at night, when this building is as quiet as a ship, do I feel the words inside me float to the surface.' I asked how many poems he'd written. He flipped through and said roughly three hundred. Three hundred poems, all in the same blue notebook, never shown to anyone. I asked why he didn't post them online. He shook his head and said: 'Writing poetry isn't for others to read. It's for keeping myself company through those quiet nights.'

title: The Alley That Changes with the Seasons
text_zh: 我租的房子在一条老巷子里。这条巷子最有意思的地方，不是建筑本身，而是它会随着季节散发不同的味道。春天的时候，巷口那棵老槐树开花了，整条巷子都是甜丝丝的香味，走在里面像走进了一罐蜂蜜。夏天，各家各户把凉席和竹椅搬到门口乘凉，空气里弥漫着花露水和蚊香的味道，偶尔还有西瓜的甜味从某扇开着的窗户飘出来。秋天是桂花的季节，巷子尽头有一棵桂花树，每次经过都会下意识地深呼吸一下。冬天最特别——各家炖肉、煮汤的味道从厨房窗户飘出来，混合在冷空气里，让人还没到家就已经觉得暖和了。有一次我跟朋友说起这件事，他说：「你住的不是巷子，是一本关于气味的日历。」我觉得他说得太准确了。城市在不断翻新，很多巷子已经被拆掉了。但味道是拆不掉的——它存在于记忆里，比任何建筑都持久。即使有一天这条巷子也消失了，我想我还是能闭上眼睛，准确地说出每个季节它闻起来是什么样子。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zū de fángzi zài yī tiáo lǎo xiàngzi lǐ. Zhè tiáo xiàngzi zuì yǒu yìsi de dìfang, bú shì jiànzhù běnshēn, ér shì tā huì suí zhe jìjié sànfā bùtóng de wèidao. Chūntiān de shíhou, xiàngkǒu nà kē lǎo huáishù kāi huā le, zhěng tiáo xiàngzi dōu shì tiánsīsī de xiāngwèi, zǒu zài lǐmiàn xiàng zǒu jìn le yī guàn fēngmì. Xiàtiān, gè jiā gè hù bǎ liángxí hé zhúyǐ bān dào ménkǒu chéngliáng, kōngqì lǐ mímàn zhe huālùshuǐ hé wénxiāng de wèidao, ǒu'ěr hái yǒu xīguā de tiánwèi cóng mǒu shàn kāi zhe de chuānghù piāo chūlái. Qiūtiān shì guìhuā de jìjié, xiàngzi jìntóu yǒu yī kē guìhuā shù, měi cì jīngguò dōu huì xià yìshi de shēn hūxī yīxià. Dōngtiān zuì tèbié——gè jiā dùn ròu, zhǔ tāng de wèidao cóng chúfáng chuānghù piāo chūlái, hùnhé zài lěng kōngqì lǐ, ràng rén hái méi dào jiā jiù yǐjīng juéde nuǎnhuo le. Yǒu yī cì wǒ gēn péngyou shuō qǐ zhè jiàn shì, tā shuō: 「Nǐ zhù de bú shì xiàngzi, shì yī běn guānyú qìwèi de rìlì.」Wǒ juéde tā shuō de tài zhǔnquè le. Chéngshì zài búduàn fānxīn, hěn duō xiàngzi yǐjīng bèi chāi diào le. Dàn wèidao shì chāi bú diào de——tā cúnzài yú jìyì lǐ, bǐ rènhé jiànzhù dōu chíjiǔ. Jíshǐ yǒu yī tiān zhè tiáo xiàngzi yě xiāoshī le, wǒ xiǎng wǒ háishi néng bì shàng yǎnjīng, zhǔnquè de shuō chū měi gè jìjié tā wén qǐlái shì shénme yàngzi.
text_en: The apartment I rent is on an old alley. The most interesting thing about this alley isn't the architecture itself, but that it gives off different smells with each season. In spring, the old locust tree at the alley entrance blooms, filling the whole lane with a faintly sweet fragrance—walking through it is like stepping into a jar of honey. In summer, every household moves bamboo mats and chairs to their doorsteps to cool off, and the air is thick with the scent of floral water and mosquito coils, with an occasional sweetness of watermelon drifting from some open window. Autumn is osmanthus season; there's an osmanthus tree at the end of the alley, and every time I pass I unconsciously take a deep breath. Winter is the most special—the smells of braised meat and simmering soup drift from kitchen windows, mixing with the cold air, making you feel warm before you've even reached home. Once I mentioned this to a friend, and he said: 'You don't live in an alley—you live in a calendar of smells.' I thought that was perfectly put. Cities keep renovating, and many alleys have already been torn down. But smells can't be torn down—they exist in memory, more enduring than any building. Even if one day this alley disappears too, I think I could still close my eyes and accurately describe what each season smelled like.

title: The Name I Don't Use Anymore
text_zh: 我有两个名字。一个是身份证上的，是爷爷起的，四个字，带着明显的客家味道。另一个是上小学时老师给改的，两个字，简单好记，同学们都叫这个。后来工作、社交、所有的证件上用的都是身份证上的名字，但心里总觉得那两个字才是「真正的我」。有一次回老家过年，一个多年没见的邻居叫了我一声小时候的名字，我整个人愣了一下，然后不自觉地笑了。那一刻我发现，名字不只是一个符号——它像一把钥匙，能打开特定时期的记忆。身份证上的名字打开的是成年后的世界：工作、合同、银行账户。而那两个字打开的是另一个世界：下课后在田埂上跑、夏天在河里游泳、爷爷坐在门口抽旱烟。两个名字，两段人生，装在同一个人的身体里。现在会叫我那个名字的人越来越少了，老邻居们搬的搬、老的老。有时候我会想，如果有一天再也没有人叫那个名字了，那段记忆是不是也会跟着消失？还是说，它只是安静地睡着了，等待某一天某个声音把它唤醒？
text_pinyin: Wǒ yǒu liǎng gè míngzi. Yī gè shì shēnfènzhèng shàng de, shì yéye qǐ de, sì gè zì, dài zhe míngxiǎn de Kèjiā wèidao. Lìng yī gè shì shàng xiǎoxué shí lǎoshī gěi gǎi de, liǎng gè zì, jiǎndān hǎo jì, tóngxuémen dōu jiào zhège. Hòulái gōngzuò, shèjiāo, suǒyǒu de zhèngjiàn shàng yòng de dōu shì shēnfènzhèng shàng de míngzi, dàn xīnlǐ zǒng juéde nà liǎng gè zì cái shì 「zhēnzhèng de wǒ」. Yǒu yī cì huí lǎojiā guònián, yī gè duō nián méi jiàn de línjū jiào le wǒ yī shēng xiǎo shíhou de míngzi, wǒ zhěnggè rén lèng le yīxià, ránhòu bù zìjué de xiào le. Nà yī kè wǒ fāxiàn, míngzi bù zhǐ shì yī gè fúhào——tā xiàng yī bǎ yàoshi, néng dǎkāi tèdìng shíqī de jìyì. Shēnfènzhèng shàng de míngzi dǎkāi de shì chéngnián hòu de shìjiè: gōngzuò, hétong, yínháng zhànghù. Ér nà liǎng gè zì dǎkāi de shì lìng yī gè shìjiè: xiàkè hòu zài tiángěng shàng pǎo, xiàtiān zài hé lǐ yóuyǒng, yéye zuò zài ménkǒu chōu hànyān. Liǎng gè míngzi, liǎng duàn rénshēng, zhuāng zài tóngyī gè rén de shēntǐ lǐ. Xiànzài huì jiào wǒ nàge míngzi de rén yuè lái yuè shǎo le, lǎo línjūmen bān de bān, lǎo de lǎo. Yǒu shíhou wǒ huì xiǎng, rúguǒ yǒu yī tiān zài yě méiyǒu rén jiào nàge míngzi le, nà duàn jìyì shì bú shì yě huì gēn zhe xiāoshī? Háishi shuō, tā zhǐshì ānjìng de shuì zháo le, děngdài mǒu yī tiān mǒu gè shēngyīn bǎ tā huànxǐng?
text_en: I have two names. One is on my ID card, given by my grandfather—four characters, with a distinctly Hakka flavor. The other was changed by my elementary school teacher—two characters, simple and easy to remember. All my classmates called me by that one. Later, for work, socializing, and all official documents I used the name on my ID, but in my heart I always felt those two characters were the 'real me.' Once when I went back to my hometown for Spring Festival, a neighbor I hadn't seen in years called out my childhood name. My whole body froze for a moment, then I smiled involuntarily. In that instant I discovered that a name isn't just a symbol—it's like a key that can unlock memories of a specific era. The name on my ID unlocks the adult world: work, contracts, bank accounts. Those two characters unlock another world: running along the rice paddy ridges after school, swimming in the river in summer, Grandpa sitting by the door smoking his pipe. Two names, two lifetimes, housed in the same body. Now fewer and fewer people call me by that name—the old neighbors have moved away or aged. Sometimes I wonder: if one day nobody ever calls that name again, will those memories disappear along with it? Or are they just quietly sleeping, waiting for some voice someday to wake them?

title: The Tree That Nobody Planted
text_zh: 我们小区的停车场角落有一棵树，谁也说不清它是什么品种，也没人记得它是什么时候长出来的。物业说那个位置从来没有种过树，应该是种子被风吹到了墙缝里，自己长起来的。这棵树就这样在水泥和砖块的夹缝中生存了下来，歪歪扭扭的，但居然长得还不错，夏天的时候能撑出一小片阴凉。停车场的保安老周对这棵树有一种特殊的感情。他说他刚来的时候，这棵树还只有一米高，现在已经快四米了。他会定期给它浇水，冬天的时候还在树根周围铺了一些干草。有人跟物业投诉说这棵树的根可能会破坏地面，建议砍掉。老周知道以后，专门去找了物业经理，说：「这棵树谁也没种过，它是自己选择长在这里的。连它自己都没放弃，我们为什么要替它做这个决定？」后来物业没有砍树，而是在树周围修了一个小花坛，还放了一条长椅。现在那里成了老人们最喜欢的乘凉地点。一棵没人种过的树，最终为自己争取到了一个家。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen xiǎoqū de tíngchēchǎng jiǎoluò yǒu yī kē shù, shuí yě shuō bù qīng tā shì shénme pǐnzhǒng, yě méi rén jìde tā shì shénme shíhou zhǎng chūlái de. Wùyè shuō nàge wèizhì cónglái méiyǒu zhòng guò shù, yīnggāi shì zhǒngzi bèi fēng chuī dào le qiángfèng lǐ, zìjǐ zhǎng qǐlái de. Zhè kē shù jiù zhèyàng zài shuǐní hé zhuānkuài de jiāfèng zhōng shēngcún le xiàlái, wāiwāiniūniū de, dàn jūrán zhǎng de hái búcuò, xiàtiān de shíhou néng chēng chū yī xiǎo piàn yīnliáng. Tíngchēchǎng de bǎo'ān lǎo Zhōu duì zhè kē shù yǒu yī zhǒng tèshū de gǎnqíng. Tā shuō tā gāng lái de shíhou, zhè kē shù hái zhǐ yǒu yī mǐ gāo, xiànzài yǐjīng kuài sì mǐ le. Tā huì dìngqī gěi tā jiāo shuǐ, dōngtiān de shíhou hái zài shùgēn zhōuwéi pū le yīxiē gāncǎo. Yǒu rén gēn wùyè tóusù shuō zhè kē shù de gēn kěnéng huì pòhuài dìmiàn, jiànyì kǎn diào. Lǎo Zhōu zhīdào yǐhòu, zhuānmén qù zhǎo le wùyè jīnglǐ, shuō: 「Zhè kē shù shuí yě méi zhòng guò, tā shì zìjǐ xuǎnzé zhǎng zài zhèlǐ de. Lián tā zìjǐ dōu méi fàngqì, wǒmen wèishéme yào tì tā zuò zhège juédìng?」Hòulái wùyè méiyǒu kǎn shù, ér shì zài shù zhōuwéi xiū le yī gè xiǎo huātán, hái fàng le yī tiáo chángyǐ. Xiànzài nàlǐ chéng le lǎorénmen zuì xǐhuan de chéngliáng dìdiǎn. Yī kē méi rén zhòng guò de shù, zuìzhōng wèi zìjǐ zhēngqǔ dào le yī gè jiā.
text_en: In the corner of our compound's parking lot there's a tree. Nobody can say what species it is, and no one remembers when it first appeared. Property management says no tree was ever planted there—a seed must have blown into a crack in the wall and grown on its own. The tree survived this way, wedged between concrete and brick, growing crooked but surprisingly well, managing to spread a small patch of shade in summer. Old Zhou, the parking lot security guard, has a special attachment to this tree. He says when he first arrived it was only a meter tall; now it's nearly four. He waters it regularly, and in winter he spreads dried grass around its roots. Someone complained to property management that the tree's roots might damage the pavement and suggested cutting it down. When Old Zhou heard, he went specifically to the property manager and said: 'Nobody planted this tree. It chose to grow here itself. Even it hasn't given up—why should we make that decision for it?' In the end, management didn't cut the tree. Instead they built a small flower bed around it and placed a bench there. Now it's become the elderly residents' favorite spot to enjoy the shade. A tree nobody planted eventually won itself a home.

questions.q_zh: 这棵树是怎么来的？
questions.q_en: How did this tree come to be?

options.text_en: Property management planted it

options.text: 自然的力量比人类强大

title: The Elevator Etiquette Incident
text_zh: 我们这栋楼只有两部电梯，早上八点是最挤的时候。大家都在赶时间上班，电梯门一开，所有人都往里冲。有一天早上，住在十八楼的赵叔按住开门键让一位抱着孩子的年轻妈妈先进去，结果后面的人嫌他耽误时间，小声嘀咕了几句。赵叔没说什么，但第二天他在电梯里贴了一张手写的告示：「早高峰电梯使用建议：一、抱小孩的优先。二、拿很多东西的优先。三、赶飞机的优先（请提前说明）。四、以上都不符合的，请微笑排队。」告示贴出去以后，效果出人意料。不是说大家真的按照规则来了，而是每天早上等电梯的时候，大家会站在那里读一遍，然后互相笑一下。有人补充了第五条：「带狗的请走楼梯，因为狗会按所有的楼层按钮。」又有人加了第六条：「如果你昨晚吃了大蒜，请等下一趟。」告示越来越长，语气越来越荒诞，但电梯里的气氛却越来越好了。赵叔后来跟我说：「我本来想教大家礼仪，结果变成了一个笑话集。但你想想，能让陌生人一起笑的东西，本身不就是最好的礼仪吗？」
text_pinyin: Wǒmen zhè dòng lóu zhǐ yǒu liǎng bù diàntī, zǎoshang bā diǎn shì zuì jǐ de shíhou. Dàjiā dōu zài gǎn shíjiān shàngbān, diàntī mén yī kāi, suǒyǒu rén dōu wǎng lǐ chōng. Yǒu yī tiān zǎoshang, zhù zài shíbā lóu de Zhào shū àn zhù kāimén jiàn ràng yī wèi bào zhe háizi de niánqīng māma xiān jìnqù, jiéguǒ hòumiàn de rén xián tā dānwù shíjiān, xiǎo shēng dīgu le jǐ jù. Zhào shū méi shuō shénme, dàn dì'èr tiān tā zài diàntī lǐ tiē le yī zhāng shǒuxiě de gàoshi: 「Zǎo gāofēng diàntī shǐyòng jiànyì: yī, bào xiǎohái de yōuxiān. Èr, ná hěn duō dōngxi de yōuxiān. Sān, gǎn fēijī de yōuxiān (qǐng tíqián shuōmíng). Sì, yǐshàng dōu bù fúhé de, qǐng wēixiào páiduì.」Gàoshi tiē chūqù yǐhòu, xiàoguǒ chū rén yìliào. Bú shì shuō dàjiā zhēn de ànzhào guīzé lái le, ér shì měi tiān zǎoshang děng diàntī de shíhou, dàjiā huì zhàn zài nàlǐ dú yī biàn, ránhòu hùxiāng xiào yīxià. Yǒu rén bǔchōng le dì wǔ tiáo: 「Dài gǒu de qǐng zǒu lóutī, yīnwèi gǒu huì àn suǒyǒu de lóucéng ànniǔ.」Yòu yǒu rén jiā le dì liù tiáo: 「Rúguǒ nǐ zuówǎn chī le dàsuàn, qǐng děng xià yī tàng.」Gàoshi yuè lái yuè cháng, yǔqì yuè lái yuè huāngdàn, dàn diàntī lǐ de qìfēn què yuè lái yuè hǎo le. Zhào shū hòulái gēn wǒ shuō: 「Wǒ běnlái xiǎng jiào dàjiā lǐyí, jiéguǒ biànchéng le yī gè xiàohua jí. Dàn nǐ xiǎng xiǎng, néng ràng mòshēngrén yīqǐ xiào de dōngxi, běnshēn bú jiùshì zuì hǎo de lǐyí ma?」
text_en: Our building has only two elevators, and 8 AM is the most crowded time. Everyone is rushing to work, and the moment the elevator doors open, people charge in. One morning, Uncle Zhao from the 18th floor held the door-open button to let a young mother carrying a child go first, but people behind him grumbled about the delay. Uncle Zhao said nothing, but the next day he posted a handwritten notice in the elevator: 'Morning rush elevator usage suggestions: 1. Those carrying small children go first. 2. Those carrying many things go first. 3. Those catching a flight go first (please state in advance). 4. If none of the above apply, please smile and queue.' The effect after posting was unexpected. Not that everyone actually followed the rules, but each morning while waiting for the elevator, people would stand there reading through the list, then share a laugh. Someone added a fifth rule: 'Those with dogs, please take the stairs, because dogs press every floor button.' Another added a sixth: 'If you ate garlic last night, please wait for the next one.' The notice grew longer and the tone more absurd, but the atmosphere in the elevator kept improving. Uncle Zhao later told me: 'I originally wanted to teach everyone etiquette, but it turned into a joke collection. But think about it—isn't something that can make strangers laugh together the best etiquette of all?'
